title: Erdős–Hajnal Conjecture (Conjecture 1)
statement_text: For every positive integers $k$ and $g$, there exists an integer $h(k,g)$ such that every graph $G$ with $\chi(G) \geq h(k,g)$ contains a subgraph with chromatic number at least $k$ and girth at least $g$.
context_text: Proposed by Erdős and Hajnal in the 1960s, this conjecture generalises the existence of graphs with large girth and large chromatic number to a Ramsey-type statement. The only non-trivial confirmed case is $g=4$ (triangle-free subgraphs), proved by Rödl in 1977. The paper also notes it holds for Kneser graphs (Theorem 16, Corollary 17).
paper_contribution: Proves the fractional chromatic number analogue of Rödl's $g=4$ case (Theorem 3): for every real $x \geq 1$ there exists $k(x)$ such that every graph $G$ with $\chi_f(G) \geq k(x)$ contains a triangle-free subgraph $H$ with $\chi_f(H) \geq x$. Also verifies the conjecture for Kneser graphs.
notes: PDF source — the extracted statement text is partially garbled (the quantifier clause 'such that every graph $G$ with $\chi(G) \geq h(k,g)$' is missing from the raw extraction); statement has been reconstructed from context.
paper_title: Triangle-free subgraphs with large fractional chromatic number

title: Conjecture 4
statement_text: For every real number $x \geq 1$ and any integer $g \geq 3$, there exists a positive number $k(x,g)$ such that every graph $G$ with $\chi_f(G) \geq k(x,g)$ contains a subgraph $H$ of girth at least $g$ and with $\chi_f(H) \geq x$.
context_text: Proposed immediately after Theorem 3 as the natural full generalisation to arbitrary girth. Theorem 3 of this paper establishes the case $g = 4$ (triangle-free subgraphs), and Conjecture 4 asks whether the same holds for every girth.
paper_title: Triangle-free subgraphs with large fractional chromatic number

title: Problem 1 (Bukh)
statement_text: Does there exist a constant $c$ so that for every graph $G$, $\mathbb{E}(\chi(G_{1/2})) > c\,\dfrac{\chi(G)}{\log \chi(G)}$?
context_text: Asked by Boris Bukh as a weaker form of whether a random subgraph of a graph with large chromatic number retains comparably large chromatic number. Here $G_p$ denotes the random subgraph of $G$ in which each edge is retained independently with probability $p$.
paper_contribution: The methods developed in this paper led to a solution of the fractional chromatic number version of this problem, established in the companion paper [11] (Mohar–Wu).
notes: PDF source — math appears readable. The paper does not directly prove this problem; it solves the fractional version in a separate companion paper.
paper_title: Triangle-free subgraphs with large fractional chromatic number

title: Thomassen's Conjecture (Conjecture 5)
statement_text: For every positive integers $k$ and $g \geq 3$, there exists a positive number $d(k,g)$ such that every graph with average degree at least $d(k,g)$ contains a subgraph of girth at least $g$ and average degree at least $k$.
context_text: Proposed by Carsten Thomassen in 1983, this conjecture asks whether sufficiently high average degree forces a subgraph of prescribed girth and average degree. Partial results are known for $g \leq 6$ (Kühn–Osthus) and under bounded maximum-degree assumptions (Pyber et al.; Dellamonica–Rödl).
paper_contribution: The paper does not prove this conjecture; it introduces Conjecture 6 as a weakening (replacing average degree in the hypothesis by chromatic number) that combines this conjecture with the Erdős–Hajnal framework.
notes: PDF source — math appears readable. Mentioned as motivation for Conjecture 6; no direct progress on this conjecture is made in the paper.
paper_title: Triangle-free subgraphs with large fractional chromatic number

title: Conjecture 6
statement_text: For every positive integers $k$ and $g \geq 3$, there exists a positive number $c(k,g)$ such that every graph with chromatic number at least $c(k,g)$ contains a subgraph of girth at least $g$ and with average degree at least $k$.
context_text: Proposed by the authors as a weakening of both the Erdős–Hajnal conjecture and Thomassen's conjecture: the hypothesis uses chromatic number (rather than average degree) and the conclusion asks only for large average degree (rather than large chromatic number) in the subgraph.
paper_title: Triangle-free subgraphs with large fractional chromatic number

title: Conjecture 7
statement_text: For every positive integers $k$ and $g$, there exists an integer $h(k,g)$ such that every graph $G$ with $\chi(G) \geq h(k,g)$ contains a subgraph with chromatic number at least $k$ and odd girth at least $g$.
context_text: An odd-girth variant of the Erdős–Hajnal conjecture. The paper notes that the analogous statement for Thomassen's conjecture (with odd girth replacing girth) is trivially true, making this chromatic-number version a natural open problem.
paper_title: Triangle-free subgraphs with large fractional chromatic number
